overview: Ethereum began when teenage Bitcoin writer Vitalik Buterin saw that separate blockchain projects could become one programmable base layer. His 2013 white paper specified a decentralized virtual machine whose smart contracts could represent currencies, organizations, markets, and applications. A distributed founding group formed a Swiss foundation, funded development through an Ether crowdsale, and launched in 2015. Unlike Bitcoin's narrow monetary design, Ethereum became an evolving ‘world computer’ shaped by developers, token holders, miners, and social consensus.

Ethereum's earliest killer application was fundraising: ERC-20 tokens powered an ICO boom that bootstrapped liquidity, users, and infrastructure before collapsing. The DAO hack forced a human choice to reverse theft through a hard fork, leaving Ethereum Classic. CryptoKitties introduced NFTs; Maker, Uniswap, and Compound assembled decentralized finance. Ethereum's developer lead, standards, liquidity, and legitimacy create switching costs, but congestion, gas fees, slow governance, competing chains, regulation, and the hazardous transition toward proof of stake threaten its promise.

takeaways.title: General platforms absorb specialized products
takeaways.detail: Buterin noticed that colored coins, domain systems, and financial protocols were each adding narrow logic to Bitcoin. A programmable virtual machine moved that logic into applications, letting one network support uses its creators could not anticipate and turning developer creativity into the product roadmap.

takeaways.title: Legitimacy is a network asset
takeaways.detail: Ethereum and Ethereum Classic share ancestry and compatible technology, yet their values diverged because developers, exchanges, applications, and users coordinated around one chain. History, visible stewardship, standards, and collective belief compound into brand and switching costs that a technically similar fork cannot copy.

takeaways.title: Speculation can bootstrap useful infrastructure
takeaways.detail: ICOs brought dubious projects and severe losses, but also created wallets, exchanges, token standards, capital, and a global user base. Like Silk Road's role in Bitcoin, an initially problematic use case financed and stress-tested infrastructure later used for NFTs, lending, and decentralized exchange.

takeaways.title: Immutability ultimately includes human consensus
takeaways.detail: The DAO exploit followed deployed code, yet the community chose to fork and restore funds. Ethereum therefore combines cryptographic execution with weak subjectivity: software determines state until enough participants agree that exceptional circumstances justify changing the rules.

takeaways.title: Success can degrade a shared network
takeaways.detail: More applications and users increased Ethereum's economic value but also drove gas fees above practical levels and reduced utility. Network effects coexist with diseconomies when every node processes the same state. Scaling must preserve security and composability without destroying the legitimacy that created the lead.

chapters.title: A teenage writer enters Bitcoin
chapters.summary: Seventeen-year-old Vitalik Buterin joins BitcoinTalk under his real name and earns five Bitcoin per article. His clear technical writing builds a reputation and leads to co-founding the print publication Bitcoin Magazine.
chapters.quote: he wants to write about Bitcoin

chapters.title: Travel reveals fragmented blockchain ambition
chapters.summary: After leaving university, Buterin visits crypto projects and helps draft their white papers. Colored coins, Mastercoin, and other specialized protocols convince him that a general programming layer can replace repeated one-off designs.
chapters.quote: these things are all separate projects

chapters.title: Two weeks produce the world-computer blueprint
chapters.summary: Couch-surfing in San Francisco, Buterin writes Ethereum's white paper and defines a virtual machine for arbitrary smart contracts and decentralized applications. Ether will pay for computation and secure the network.
chapters.quote: he writes the white paper in two weeks

chapters.title: A distributed founding team assembles
chapters.summary: Early supporters add financing, operations, and engineering: Charles Hoskinson becomes CEO, Gavin Wood implements the C++ client and formal specification, and Joseph Lubin joins around Ethereum's Miami debut.

chapters.title: Foundation governance replaces a conventional startup
chapters.summary: Conflicting plans for a venture-backed company, control, compensation, and mission erupt among the founders. Buterin chooses a Swiss nonprofit foundation, removes several early leaders, and prioritizes an open protocol over equity ownership.

chapters.title: A crowdsale finances the network
chapters.summary: The foundation sells Ether before launch to fund development, giving future users the token required to pay gas. The mechanism precedes clear securities guidance and becomes the model for later token offerings.

chapters.title: Ethereum launches before its promised future
chapters.summary: The Frontier network goes live in 2015, but the planned rapid progression to scalable proof of stake does not. The gap between functioning experimentation and repeatedly delayed Serenity becomes a persistent governance and execution challenge.
chapters.quote: All five phases in the first year was the plan

chapters.title: ICOs bootstrap demand and excess
chapters.summary: Token creation becomes Ethereum's first killer application: 64 offerings raise over $100 million in 2016, then 966 raise more than $10 billion in 2017. Speculation grows network infrastructure before the market collapses.
chapters.quote: ICOs were the killer use case

chapters.title: The DAO exploit tests immutability
chapters.summary: A smart-contract organization accumulates roughly 14% of existing Ether before an attacker drains funds through a code vulnerability. Exchanges pause trading while the community debates whether code alone should determine the outcome.

chapters.title: A hard fork creates Ethereum Classic
chapters.summary: Most participants adopt a fork that restores the DAO funds, while immutability advocates continue the original ledger as Ethereum Classic. The split proves that social coordination selects the economically legitimate chain.

chapters.title: NFTs and DeFi find productive composability
chapters.summary: CryptoKitties popularizes unique digital assets and exposes capacity limits. Maker enables stable collateral, Uniswap creates permissionless liquidity pools, and Compound adds lending incentives, allowing independent contracts to combine into financial services.

chapters.title: Ethereum's lead faces a scaling wager
chapters.summary: Developers, ERC-20 assets, liquidity, trading pairs, and accumulated legitimacy create intertwined switching costs, but congestion also produces negative network effects. Proof of stake and scaling upgrades must expand capacity without breaking the security and community trust that make migration difficult.
chapters.quote: a four or five-year lead with the developer community

title: Ethereum: Vitalik Buterin, the DAO, DeFi, and the World Computer
